editorial.title: The rake of wrath: green street’s nicotine wars
editorial.teaser: Professor remy explores the devolution of neighbourhood relations, where a single cigarette butt can transform a horticulturalist into a medieval pikeman.
editorial.body:
Ah, the gentle art of gardening. The scent of fresh mulch, the soothing hum of the bees, and the sickening thud of a metal rake making contact with a neighbour’s torso.

it appears the social contract has finally snapped on green street, and it didn’t break over ideology, property values, or a fence line dispute. No, civilization crumbled over the ballistic trajectory of a single spent cigarette.

we are told that a gardener—custodian of the verdant verge—decided that a discarded "durry" was not merely litter, but a declaration of total war. And honestly, looking at the state of modern manners, who among us hasn’t felt the primal urge to go medieval while weeding?

the weapon of choice was not a stern word, nor a passive-aggressive note left in a letterbox (the traditional ammunition of the suburbs), but a rake. A tool designed to gather autumn leaves was instantaneously repurposed as a trident of suburban vengeance.

it is a fascinating, if horrifying, escalation. One moment you are pruning hydrangeas, the next you are recreating a gladiator match because someone treated the footpath like an oversized ashtray.

the police, naturally, were called to referee this bout of heavyweight horticulture. One imagines the dispatch call: "suspect is armed with a leaf-gathering implement and extreme prejudice." it speaks to the simmering rage beneath our manicured hedges.

we are apparently one un-binned wrapper away from anarchy. Is the cigarette butt the most offensive artifact of modern life?

perhaps to the botanical purist. But does it warrant a pronged assault?

in the kangaroo court of green street, the verdict was swift and kinetic. As the sirens wailed, we were left to ponder: are we civilized neighbours, or are we just savages with better lawns?

next time, just use the bin. It’s safer for everyone’s shins.
editorial.author: Professor Remy
